name: prompt-caching
context: Cache prompt prefixes to reduce latency and cost on repeated context like system prompts and documents
info: Prompt caching lets you mark portions of your prompt for reuse across API calls. On the first request, cached content is written at 1.25x the base input price (5-minute TTL) or 2x (1-hour TTL). Subsequent requests that match the cached prefix read from cache at just 0.1x the base price — a 90% discount on those tokens. This is essential for applications with large system prompts, uploaded documents, extensive tool definitions, or long conversation histories. Two approaches: automatic caching (single top-level cache_control, system manages breakpoints) and explicit breakpoints (cache_control on individual content blocks, up to 4 breakpoints). Minimum cacheable lengths vary by model: 1024-4096 tokens. Track performance via usage fields: cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, and input_tokens.
triggers: cache a large document for claude
body:
# Prompt Caching

Prompt caching avoids reprocessing identical prompt prefixes on every API call. Cache once, read many times at 10% of the base input price.

## Automatic Caching (Recommended)

Add a single `cache_control` at the top level. The system manages breakpoints automatically:

```python
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    cache_control={"type": "ephemeral"},
    system="You are an expert assistant. Here is the full project documentation: [50,000 tokens of docs...]",
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "How do I deploy the application?"}
    ]
)

# Check cache performance
print(f"Cache write: {response.usage.cache_creation_input_tokens}")
print(f"Cache read: {response.usage.cache_read_input_tokens}")
print(f"Uncached: {response.usage.input_tokens}")
```

## Explicit Cache Breakpoints

Place `cache_control` on specific content blocks (up to 4 breakpoints):

```python
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    system=[
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": "You are an AI assistant analyzing legal documents.",
            "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
        },
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": "[Full 50-page legal agreement...]",
            "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
        }
    ],
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "What are the key terms?"}
    ]
)
```

## Cache Tool Definitions

```python
tools = [
    {"name": "tool1", "description": "...", "input_schema": {...}},
    {
        "name": "tool2",
        "description": "...",
        "input_schema": {...},
        "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
    }
]
```

## TTL Options

| TTL | Write Cost | Read Cost | Use When |
|-----|-----------|-----------|----------|
| 5 minutes (default) | 1.25x base | 0.1x base | Frequent requests within 5 min |
| 1 hour | 2x base | 0.1x base | Less frequent but regular requests |

```python
# 5-minute cache (default)
{"cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}}

# 1-hour cache
{"cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral", "ttl": "1h"}}
```

## Minimum Cacheable Lengths

| Model | Min Tokens |
|-------|------------|
| Claude Opus 4.6, Opus 4.5, Haiku 4.5 | 4096 |
| Claude Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 3.5 | 2048 |
| Claude Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.1, Opus 4, Sonnet 4 | 1024 |

## Pricing by Model

| Model | Base Input | Cache Write (5m) | Cache Write (1h) | Cache Read |
|-------|-----------|------------------|-------------------|------------|
| Opus 4.6 | $5/MTok | $6.25/MTok | $10/MTok | $0.50/MTok |
| Sonnet 4.6 | $3/MTok | $3.75/MTok | $6/MTok | $0.30/MTok |
| Haiku 4.5 | $1/MTok | $1.25/MTok | $2/MTok | $0.10/MTok |

## Cache Hit Rate Impact on Rate Limits

Cached tokens do NOT count toward ITPM rate limits (on most models). This means effective throughput scales dramatically with caching. Example: with 2M ITPM limit and 80% cache rate, you can process ~10M total input tokens per minute.

## Multi-Turn Conversation Caching

With automatic caching, the system moves the breakpoint forward as conversations grow:

| Request | Cached | New |
|---------|--------|-----|
| Request 1 | — | System + User(1) written to cache |
| Request 2 | System + User(1) from cache | Asst(1) + User(2) written to cache |
| Request 3 | System → User(2) from cache | Asst(2) + User(3) written to cache |

## Cache Invalidation

Changes to earlier content invalidate downstream cache entries:
- Changing tool definitions invalidates everything
- Changing system prompt invalidates system + messages cache
- Changing tool_choice only affects message blocks

## Best Practices

1. Start with automatic caching for conversations
2. Use explicit breakpoints when sections change at different rates
3. Place stable content (system prompt, docs) before variable content
4. Cache tool definitions if you send the same tools repeatedly
5. Monitor `cache_creation_input_tokens` and `cache_read_input_tokens` to verify hits
6. Keep < 20 new blocks per turn for consistent cache hits

sections.title: Automatic caching
sections.body: Add `cache_control: {type: 'ephemeral'}` at the request top level. The system places the breakpoint on the last cacheable block and moves it forward as conversations grow. Best for multi-turn conversations where you want zero-effort caching. The system handles breakpoint management automatically.

sections.title: Explicit cache breakpoints
sections.body: Place `cache_control` directly on content blocks for fine-grained control. Up to 4 breakpoints per request. Cache writes happen only at breakpoint positions. The system checks up to 20 positions backward for cache matches. Place breakpoints on the last block whose prefix is identical across requests.

sections.title: Cache pricing
sections.body: 5-minute cache writes cost 1.25x base input. 1-hour writes cost 2x base. Cache reads cost 0.1x base — a 90% discount. Break-even: one read for 5m cache, two reads for 1h cache. Pricing stacks with batch API discount and data residency multiplier. Track via usage fields: cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens.

sections.title: Rate limit optimization
sections.body: On most models, cache_read_input_tokens do NOT count toward ITPM rate limits. Only uncached input_tokens and cache_creation_input_tokens count. This means prompt caching effectively multiplies your rate limit capacity — an 80% cache hit rate gives 5x effective throughput.

scripts.name: Cached Conversation
scripts.description: Multi-turn conversation with automatic prompt caching
scripts.inline:
import anthropic
import os

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
model = os.environ.get("ANTHROPIC_MODEL", "claude-sonnet-4-6")

system_prompt = "You are an expert Python developer. Provide concise, production-quality code. Always include error handling and type hints."

messages = []
print("Cached conversation demo (type 'quit' to exit)")
print("---")

while True:
    user_input = input("You: ")
    if user_input.lower() == "quit":
        break

    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_input})

    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=2048,
        cache_control={"type": "ephemeral"},
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=messages
    )

    assistant_text = response.content[0].text
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_text})

    print(f"\nClaude: {assistant_text}")
    print(f"  [cache_write={response.usage.cache_creation_input_tokens}, cache_read={response.usage.cache_read_input_tokens}, uncached={response.usage.input_tokens}]")
    print()
